text:
A relative of Olympic breakdancer Rachael 'Raygun' Gunn has come out in fierce defence of her performance after she failed to receive a point at the Paris Games.

Australian breakdancer Rachael 'Raygun' Gunn's performance at the Olympic Games has been staunchly defended by her father-in-law after she failed to register a point from the judges.

Competing in the first-ever Olympic women's breakdancing competition, Gunn tallied no points across all three matches in her pool against the USA, France and Lithuania.

The 36-year-old's bizarre dancing - which included hopping around like a kangaroo and doing the 'sprinkler' - made waves across the world, quickly becoming a global meme.

Gunn's father-in-law Andrew Free has hit back at the scores of the judges, suggesting she did not make it through to the finals because it was a "pretty stacked competition" and judges were "looking for a certain style of breaking which is not Rachael's".

“Although they are supposed to mark 5 different aspects with each having the same weighting, in my obviously biased opinion they did not reward originality and musicality so she was up against it," Mr Free said on Facebook.

“The main thing is she represented Australia and breaking at the Olympics with courage and dignity.

“It comes naturally for some of them, not so much for Rachael. It is part of the culture.”

Head breaking judge at the Olympics, Martin Gilian, said the scores of zero were not indicative of a terrible performance.

“It’s all about originality and it’s all about bringing something new to the table and representing your country or region,” Mr Gilian said, according to CNN.

“This is exactly what Raygun was doing, she got inspired by her surroundings, which in this case, for example, was a kangaroo… she created some original moves which could be maybe funny or entertaining for others, but for us, she basically represented breaking and hip hop.

"She was trying to be original and bring something new to the table. From our perspective, that was nothing really shocking.”

While it has been the subject of relentless mocking on social media, Gunn's performance at the Olympics has also been shrouded in controversy.

Gunn, who has a PhD in breakdancing and dance culture and works as a lecturer at Macquarie University, was accused of getting zero points on purpose for an academic study.

"Getting zero points on purpose in three rounds for an academic study subsidised by the taxpayer both at a university and Olympic level isn't funny and isn't 'having a go'," Aboriginal Australian activist and international human rights lawyer Megan Davis claimed according to The Australian.

How the Olympian managed to even qualify for the Games has been called into question by many Australians, who pondered if she was the best the country could offer to compete in the sport.

"She has made the competion a laughing stock. It's not the comedy festival. How could the Oceania judges get it so wrong using the criteria of assessment used at the Olympics," one disgruntled Aussie said on a Facebook post.

"Really, she should have known that she wasn’t up to Olympic standards!!!" another commented.

Jokes about Gunn's performance haven't shown signs of slowing down just yet, with comedian and actress Rachel Dratch portraying her in a satirical bit on The Tonight Show Starring Jimmy Fallon.

She was also namedropped in a recent concert by singer Adele, who admitted she and her friends were "s***ting ourselves laughing for nearly 24 hours" after watching the event.

title: Raygun's family hits back at judges after Aussie breakdancer failed to score a point at Paris Olympics | Sky News Australia
description: Australian breakdancer Rachael 'Raygun' Gunn's performance at the Olympic Games has been staunchly defended by her father-in-law after she failed to register a point from the judges.
